original_hypothesis.title: Bribe.withdraw Silent Failure Enables Infinite Reward Capture
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The Bribe contract's withdraw function silently fails if the requested withdrawal amount exceeds the user's recorded balance. If the Voter contract attempts to withdraw a larger amount than deposited (e.g., due to veNFT balance increases from rebases), the withdrawal is ignored, but the Voter clears the user's vote state. The user can then vote again, inflating their Bribe balance indefinitely and capturing a disproportionate share of the bribe rewards.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a known vulnerability pattern in ve(3,3) forks where weight recalculations cause desyncs between Voter and Bribe accounting. The silent failure in the Bribe contract makes this desync exploitable for infinite reward extraction.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (amount <= balanceOf[tokenId]) { ... } // silent failure if false

preconditions: Voter contract attempts to withdraw an amount greater than the user's Bribe balance (e.g., due to veNFT balance increase)

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: User can repeatedly vote and reset, inflating their balanceOf if withdraw silently fails
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker captures all bribe rewards for the pool by artificially inflating their share of the totalSupply

economic_questions: Does the Voter contract ever pass an amount > balanceOf to Bribe.withdraw?
Can a user increase their veNFT balance between vote and reset?
validation_requirements: Test vote -> increase veNFT balance -> reset -> vote sequence

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimal (enough to get a veNFT and vote)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas costs for voting and resetting
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to 100% of the bribe rewards for the targeted pool
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Multi-block (requires veNFT balance increase between vote and reset)

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Drains bribe rewards from legitimate voters

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Legitimate voters on the targeted gauge
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All bribe tokens deposited into the affected Bribe contract

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify if amount > balanceOf is reachable

validation_result.mechanism: Silent withdrawal failure causing Voter/Bribe state desynchronization
validation_result.impact_summary: Bribe.withdraw silently no-ops when amount > balanceOf[tokenId], preventing balance/supply decrements while the Voter contract proceeds as if the withdrawal succeeded. This can lead to permanently inflated balanceOf and totalSupply, corrupting reward distribution.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The Bribe.withdraw function at Bribes.sol wraps its entire state-mutating logic inside `if (amount <= balanceOf[tokenId])`. When the condition is false, the function returns successfully without reverting. The calling Voter contract has no way to detect this failure and will proceed to clear its internal vote tracking state. This creates a permanent desynchronization: the Voter believes the tokenId has no active votes, while the Bribe contract retains a non-zero balanceOf[tokenId] and an inflated totalSupply. The user can then vote again, triggering a new Bribe.deposit that adds to the existing balance, compounding the desync on every vote/reset cycle. The defect is independent of the Voter's implementation — any caller expecting withdraw to be authoritative will be misled by the silent no-op. The Voter contract source is not available in scope, so the exact conditions under which amount > balanceOf are not fully verifiable, but the code defect in Bribe.withdraw is independently confirmed.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal at Bribes.sol lines 194-203 is accurate. The withdraw function wraps all state changes in `if (amount <= balanceOf[tokenId])` with no else-branch or revert, creating a silent failure path. The deposit function (lines ~173-181) unconditionally adds to balanceOf and totalSupply. The earned function (lines ~89-115) uses balanceOf and totalSupply checkpoints for reward calculation. Together, these create a scenario where inflated balances lead to disproportionate reward capture.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: When amount > balanceOf[tokenId], the function silently succeeds without modifying any state or reverting. The Voter contract caller cannot detect this failure and will clear its vote tracking, creating a permanent desync between Voter and Bribe state. The user retains their bribe balance while appearing to have no active votes in the Voter.

deposit unconditionally adds to balanceOf[tokenId]. If a prior withdraw silently failed, re-voting causes deposit to add to the existing (non-withdrawn) balance, compounding the inflation on each vote/reset cycle.

The earned calculation uses checkpoint balanceOf divided by supply. An inflated balanceOf[tokenId] from repeated failed withdrawals + new deposits gives the tokenId a disproportionately large share of rewards, at the expense of legitimate voters.

calling_sequence: 1. User calls Voter.vote() → Voter calls Bribe.deposit(amount, tokenId) with veNFT voting power
2. Bribe.deposit increments totalSupply and balanceOf[tokenId] by amount
3. User's veNFT balance increases (rebase, deposit_for, etc.)
4. User calls Voter.reset() → Voter calls Bribe.withdraw(newAmount, tokenId) where newAmount > original amount
5. Bribe.withdraw: if(newAmount <= balanceOf[tokenId]) evaluates to false → silent no-op
6. Voter clears its internal vote state (poolVote, votes, usedWeights) regardless of Bribe outcome
7. User calls Voter.vote() again → Voter calls Bribe.deposit(newAmount, tokenId)
8. Bribe now has balanceOf[tokenId] = originalAmount + newAmount (inflated)
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerability is in Bribes.sol:194 where `if (amount <= balanceOf[tokenId])` guards all state changes. When the condition is false, the function returns successfully (no revert), creating an invisible failure. The Voter contract (out of scope, accessed via IVoter interface) calls withdraw and has no return value or revert to detect the failure. Steps 4-8 can be repeated indefinitely if the veNFT balance continues to be higher than the last deposited amount.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts affect the Bribe.withdraw logic. The defect is inherent in the contract code.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The code defect is independently confirmed: Bribe.withdraw silently fails when amount > balanceOf[tokenId]. The full attack scenario depends on the Voter contract implementation (out of scope), specifically whether the Voter passes the current veNFT balance to withdraw (which could exceed the deposited amount) or the stored vote weight (which would match). The IVoter interface shows usedWeights(uint id) suggesting stored weights may be used, but poke() functionality in many ve(3,3) implementations recalculates weights using current balances. Without the Voter source, the exact trigger conditions cannot be verified, but the Bribe code defect is real and independently dangerous — any caller mismatch will cause silent state corruption.
validation_result.impact_assessment: If the silent failure is triggered (by any cause including Voter bugs, upgrades, or edge cases), the impact is: (1) totalSupply in Bribe becomes permanently inflated relative to actual vote deposits, diluting all voters' rewards; (2) The affected tokenId's balanceOf remains elevated, granting disproportionate reward share; (3) If repeatable, the attacker captures an increasingly large share of all bribe rewards for the pool. The invariant totalSupply == sum(balanceOf[tokenId]) (INV-ECON-002) is violated.
recommended_mitigations: Replace the silent if-guard with a require or explicit revert: `require(amount <= balanceOf[tokenId], "INSUFFICIENT_BALANCE");`

Alternatively, use min(amount, balanceOf[tokenId]) to withdraw whatever is available: `uint256 withdrawAmount = Math.min(amount, balanceOf[tokenId]); totalSupply -= withdrawAmount; balanceOf[tokenId] -= withdrawAmount;`
Add a return value to withdraw() so the Voter can verify the withdrawal succeeded
Add an invariant check that totalSupply == sum of all balanceOf entries (can be done periodically off-chain)
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Bribe contract with mock Voter and VotingEscrow. 2. Call deposit(100, tokenId=1) from mock Voter. 3. Call withdraw(150, tokenId=1) from mock Voter. 4. Verify balanceOf[1] is still 100 and totalSupply is still 100 (withdrawal silently failed). 5. Call deposit(150, tokenId=1) from mock Voter. 6. Verify balanceOf[1] is 250 and totalSupply is 250 (inflated). 7. Deploy second tokenId, deposit(100, tokenId=2). 8. Notify rewards and verify tokenId=1 gets 250/350 share instead of fair 150/250.
uncertainties: The Voter contract source code is not available in scope — the exact conditions under which amount > balanceOf[tokenId] could occur in normal operation cannot be verified
Whether the Voter uses stored weights (usedWeights/votes mappings) or current veNFT balance for withdraw calls is unknown
Whether poke() or other Voter functions could trigger the mismatch is unknown
additional_testing_needed: Review the Voter contract source code (VoterV3) to determine what amount is passed to Bribe.withdraw during reset()
Test the vote → deposit_for (increase lock) → reset → vote cycle to see if the amount mismatch occurs
Verify if poke() recalculates and could cause deposit/withdraw with different amounts
Fuzz test the Voter+Bribe integration with varying veNFT balances between vote and reset
